q: A camera is an optical instrument that forms and records an image of an object.
The image formed in a camera may be recorded on film or detected by an electronic sensor.
A diaphragm is placed in front of the convex lens to control the amount of light energy falling on the film.
The aperture is a circular opening through which the light passes.
The f-number of a camera is the ratio of the focal length of the lens to the diameter of aperture.
The smaller the f-number, the wider the aperture is.
When taking a photograph in bright light a small f-number must be used.
A shutter controls the time during which light enters the camera through the lens.
The shorter the exposure time, the lower the shutter speed is.
A camera consists of a focusing ring by which the distance between the lens and the film can be adjusted to obtain a sharp image on the film.
The film at the back of the camera is a screen which receives the image.
Chemical reaction occurs when light energy falls on the film.
The image formed by a camera lens is real and inverted.
For a distant object, its image is formed at the focus of the lens.
The human eye consists of sense organs which are capable of receiving visual images.
The human eye is very different to a camera in almost all aspects.
The eyelid is similar to the lens cover of the camera.
The front portion of the eye is covered by a tough, transparent membrane called the cornea.
The region between the cornea and the eyelens is filled with a transparent liquid called the aqueous humour.
The iris is the colored portion of the eye that controls the amount of light energy entering the eye.
The pupil is in the centre of the iris and the light passes through it.
The pupil is similar to the aperture in the camera.
The pupil automatically dilates in bright light and contracts in dim light.
Ciliary muscles are attached to the eyelens.
The ciliary muscles can vary the thickness of the eyelens, thus altering the focal length.
When near objects are viewed, the ciliary muscles contract and permit the eyelens to form a more convex shape.
When distant objects are viewed, the ciliary muscles relax and the eyelens becomes less convex.
The retina is the light sensitive area of the cells at the back of the eye.
The yellow spot consists of rods exclusively.
The blind spot is where the optic nerve enters the eye.
The cornea, aqueous humour, eyelens, and vitreous humour are refracting substances.
The image of an object formed on the retina is real, inverted and smaller than the size of the object.
Farsightedness is a defect of vision in which distant objects are seen clearly, but objects nearby are blurry.
Nearsightedness is a defect of vision in which near objects are seen clearly, but objects far away are blurry.
For a person with farsightedness, the rays from near objects intersect at a point in front of the retina.
Nearsightedness can be corrected by the use of spectacles with diverging lens.
The least distance of distinct vision is about 25 cm.
For normal vision, the maximum distance of distinct vision is infinity.
A compound microscope has at least two convex lenses: an objective lens and an eyepiece lens.
In a compound microscope, the lens nearer the object is called the eyepiece.
The magnifying power of a compound microscope is calculated as the sum of the magnification of the objective and the magnifying power of the eyepiece.
For a compound microscope in normal setting, the final image is formed at infinity.
When the final image is formed at a distance of 25 cm from the eyepiece, the object distance for the eyepiece is given by $u_e = \frac{25f_e}{25+f_e}$.
In a compound microscope, the focal length of the objective should be longer than that of the eyepiece.
As the magnification of a compound microscope increases, the sharpness of the images is improved.
Electron microscopes employ electron beams in place of light.
Optical microscopes have a higher maximum magnifying power than electron microscopes.
Electron microscopes use glass lenses to focus the electron beam.
The wavelength of electrons is shorter than that of light, which allows for greater magnification in electron microscopes.
The Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) is one of the two main types of electron microscope described in the text.
In a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), the electron beam is transmitted through the specimen.
The magnifying power of an astronomical telescope is the ratio of the angle $\beta$ subtended by the image to the angle $\alpha$ subtended by the object.
A refracting astronomical telescope consists of an objective of short focal length.
The terrestrial telescope produces an inverted final image.
Binoculars use total reflecting prisms to reduce the length of the terrestrial telescope.
The Newtonian design of a reflecting telescope uses a parabolic objective mirror.
For an astronomical telescope in normal adjustment, the separation of the lenses is $L = f_o - f_e$.
Optical microscopes use photons or light energy.
The electron microscope can reach a magnification of 1,000,000.
Compound microscopes are primarily used to study the electron structure of metals and crystals.
In a compound microscope, the separation of the lenses is given by $L = v_o + u_e$.
The eyepiece of a refracting astronomical telescope has a long focal length.
Galileo invented the refracting telescope.
In a reflecting telescope, the concave mirror serves as the objective.
Binoculars consist of a pair of telescopes, one for each eye.
When the final image of an astronomical telescope is formed at infinity, the magnifying power is $MP = \frac{f_o}{f_e}$.
The magnifying power of a compound microscope in normal setting is $MP = \frac{v_o}{u_o} \times \frac{25}{f_e}$.
A terrestrial telescope uses a convex lens as an eyepiece to produce an erect image.
The electron beam in a transmission electron microscope carries information about the structure of the specimen.
For large magnifying power, the objective of a compound microscope should have a long focal length.
In a compound microscope, the magnifying power of the eyepiece is defined as $\frac{25}{u_e}$.
Electron microscopes can be used to investigate the ultrastructure of biological specimens.
The image produced by a compound microscope is real and inverted.
Reflecting telescopes are used at most astronomical observatories.
The objective of an astronomical telescope forms a real and inverted image.
In the Newtonian design, a 45 degree flat mirror is used to reflect light to the eyepiece.
Terrestrial telescopes are specifically designed to produce an erect image for viewing objects on Earth.
The magnifying power of a compound microscope is independent of the distance of the image from the eyepiece.
Electron microscopes use electromagnetic or electrostatic lenses.
The image formed by the objective lens of an astronomical telescope is at the focus of the objective.
Optics is the branch of physics that studies the nature and properties of light.
A laser is an acronym for Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.
Lasers can only produce light in the visible spectrum.
The laser medium can be a gas, liquid, or semiconductor crystal.
The power supply in a laser system is used for the pumping process.
The optical cavity consists of two mirrors with 100% reflectivity.
Population inversion is an essential process to initiate a laser action.
During stimulated emission, the radiated photons have different frequencies.
The output laser beam is characterized as being coherent.
A $1 \text{ mW}$ He-Ne laser beam is much dimmer than sunlight.
Lasers are used to destroy kidney stones.
Fibre optics works on the principle of refraction.
The refractive index of the core in an optical fibre is slightly greater than that of the cladding.
Total internal reflection occurs when the angle of incidence is less than the critical angle.
Endoscopy is a surgical procedure to examine the interior of a human body.
An optical spectrometer consists of a light source, collimator, dispersion element, and telescope.
A spectrometer is used to identify materials by their chemical make up.
The cladding of an optical fibre has a higher refractive index than the core.
Lasers are used in computer printers.
The sheath protects the optical fibre.
The wavelength of the laser mentioned in Example 7.5 is $6328 \mathring{A}$.
Stimulated emission is a process where the atom in its excited state interacts with an incident photon.
The optical cavity is also called a resonator.
For stimulated emission to occur, the population of the lower level must be higher than the upper level.
Laser beams are typically divergent and broad over short distances.
The laser medium is the material emitting the laser beam.
Laser light is monochromatic.
Spectrofluorometers are a type of spectrometer.
Total internal reflection allows light to bounce down the length of the fibre cable.
Laser light is never used in optical fibre communications.
A perfect reflector has a reflectivity of almost 100%.
Laser action is sustained when the optical cavity reflects light back and forth.
Endoscopes use optical fibres to observe an image inside the body.
The dispersion element in a spectrometer can be a prism or grating.
Lasers are used in space communication such as radars and satellites.
Refraction and absorption cause the light beam in an optical fibre to attenuate.
The core of an optical fibre is composed of two concentric layers.
In a spectrophotometer, a sample is placed between the monochromator and the detector.
The laser beam is narrow because it is coherent.
Commercial lasers can produce up to a megawatt of power.
Photometry is the science of measuring visible light in units that are weighted according to the sensitivity of the human eye.
The human eye responds to light having wavelengths between about 200 nm and 500 nm.
The human eye is more sensitive to green light of 555 nm compared to other colors.
Luminous flux is a measurement of the power of visible light produced by a light source.
One lumen is equivalent to $1/683$ watt of light with a wavelength of 555 nm.
The unit of luminous intensity is the lux.
The unit of illuminance is the candela.
A solid angle is expressed in steradian.
The solid angle subtended at the centre by the whole surface area of the sphere is $2\pi$ steradian.
Luminance is the luminous intensity per unit area of light travelling in a given direction.
The SI unit for luminance is lux.
The minimum average illuminance for school study halls should be 500 lux.
Illuminance is directly proportional to the square of the distance.
Farsightedness is a defect of vision in which distant objects are seen clearly, but objects nearby are blurry.
Nearsightedness is a defect of vision in which near objects are seen clearly, but objects far away are blurry.
A laser produces a wide beam of scattered, incoherent light.
A microscope uses lenses to make enlarged images of objects that are too small for the unaided eye to see.
The optical instrument used for viewing distant objects is called a telescope.
Luminous intensity is defined as luminous flux emitted by a light source in a direction per unit solid angle.
The wavelength range of human eye sensitivity is between 400 nm and 700 nm.
